pairs.p: music would be played on an instrument, and oars would then keep time with this
td: moved oars at the same time - keep time with (T3)

pairs.q: Quantities of agricultural goods such as [ANSWER] (grain) were transported by merchant ships to two main ports in Italy
pairs.p: agricultural products (e.g. grain from Egypt's Nile valley)... through the port of Pozzuoli... and through the gigantic port of Ostia
td: two main ports - port of Pozzuoli and port of Ostia (T4)

pairs.q: The ships were pulled to the shore by [ANSWER] (towboats)
pairs.p: Large merchant ships would approach the destination port and, just like today, be intercepted by a number of towboats that would drag them to the quay
td: pulled to the shore - drag them to the quay (T2)

pairs.q: Section D contains an explanation for weapons being left behind in the mountains
pairs.p: Hunters would have easily misplaced arrows and they often discarded broken bows rather than take them all the way home

pairs.q: A reference to the physical difficulties involved in an archaeological expedition
pairs.p: Fieldwork is hard work — hiking with all our equipment, often camping on permafrost — but very rewarding
td: physical difficulties - hard work, hiking, camping on permafrost (T2)

pairs.q: An explanation of why less food may have been available
pairs.p: A colder turn in the Scandinavian climate would likely have meant widespread crop failures
td: less food available - widespread crop failures (T3)

pairs.q: A reference to the possibility of future archaeological discoveries
pairs.p: archaeologists could be extracting some of those artefacts from retreating ice in years to come
td: future archaeological discoveries - extracting artefacts in years to come (T3)

pairs.q: Examples of items that would have been traded
pairs.p: export markets would have created a booming demand for hides to fight off the cold, as well as antlers to make useful things like combs
td: items that would have been traded - hides, antlers (T4)

pairs.q: A reference to the pressure archaeologists are under to work quickly
pairs.p: glacial archaeologists need to race the clock to find newly revealed artefacts, preserve them, and study them
td: pressure to work quickly - need to race the clock (T3)

pairs.q: Organic materials have little protection against [ANSWER] (microorganisms), which means that they decay relatively quickly
pairs.p: unless they're protected from the microorganisms that cause decay, they tend not to last long
td: little protection against - unless protected from (T6)
decay relatively quickly - tend not to last long (T3)

pairs.q: [ANSWER] (Reindeer) gathered there in the summer months
pairs.p: Reindeer once congregated on these icy patches in the later summer months

pairs.q: The reindeer gathered to avoid being attacked by [ANSWER] (insects) on lower ground
pairs.p: Reindeer once congregated on these icy patches in the later summer months to escape biting insects
td: avoid being attacked by - escape biting (T2)

pairs.q: Hunters went into the mountains even during periods of extreme cold
pairs.p: hunters kept regularly venturing into the mountains even when the climate turned cold
td: periods of extreme cold - climate turned cold (T2)

pairs.q: The number of artefacts from certain time periods was relatively low
pairs.p: they noticed a gap with almost no artefacts from about 3,800 to 2,200 BCE
td: certain time periods - about 3,800 to 2,200 BCE (T4)

pairs.q: Hunters at this time benefited from an increased demand for goods
pairs.p: export markets would have created a booming demand for hides... as well as antlers... Business must have been good for hunters
td: benefited from increased demand - booming demand, business must have been good (T3)

pairs.q: Vikings did not rely on ships alone to transport goods
pairs.p: Although we usually think of ships when we think of Scandinavian expansion, these recent discoveries show that plenty of goods travelled on overland routes
td: did not rely on ships alone - also used overland routes (T3)

pairs.q: The target for agricultural production by 2050 could be missed
pairs.p: It is estimated that agricultural yields will need to double by 2050, but climate change is a major threat to achieving this
td: target could be missed - major threat to achieving this (T3)

pairs.q: Wheat and rice suffer from a rise in temperatures
pairs.p: Key crops such as wheat and rice are sensitive to high temperatures
td: a rise in temperatures - high temperatures (T2)

pairs.q: Plants grow faster in sunlight than in shade
pairs.p: During the day, sunlight activates the molecules, slowing down growth. If a plant finds itself in shade, phytochromes are quickly inactivated — enabling it to grow faster
td: grow faster in sunlight - sunlight slows growth, shade enables faster growth (T6)

pairs.q: Phytochromes change their state at the same speed day and night
pairs.p: Light-driven changes to phytochrome activity occur very fast, in less than a second... At night, however, it's a different story. Instead of a rapid deactivation following sundown, the molecules gradually change
td: same speed day and night - very fast during day, gradually at night — a different story (T6)

pairs.q: Section H mentions specialists who can make use of the research findings
pairs.p: scientists from Germany, Argentina and the US, as well as the Cambridge team... scientists are able to rapidly identify the genes controlling these processes in crop plants
td: specialists who can make use of findings - scientists able to identify genes (T2)

pairs.q: Section D contains a reference to a potential benefit of the research findings
pairs.p: Discovering the molecules that allow plants to sense temperature has the potential to accelerate the breeding of crops resilient to thermal stress and climate change
td: potential benefit - potential to accelerate breeding of resilient crops (T2)

pairs.q: Section G provides scientific support for a traditional saying
pairs.p: the discovery of the dual role of phytochromes provides the science behind a well-known rhyme long used to predict the coming season
td: scientific support - provides the science behind (T2)
traditional saying - well-known rhyme long used (T2)

pairs.q: Section C contains a reference to people traditionally making plans based on plant behaviour
pairs.p: Farmers and gardeners have known for hundreds of years how responsive plants are to temperature... something humans have long used to predict weather and harvest times for the coming year
td: traditionally making plans - have long used to predict (T3)
based on plant behaviour - how responsive plants are to temperature (T3)

pairs.q: Section A contains a reference to where the research has been reported
pairs.p: The new findings, published in the journal Science

pairs.q: Daffodils are likely to flower early in response to [ANSWER] (warm/warm winter) weather
pairs.p: daffodils, have considerable temperature sensitivity, and can flower months in advance during a warm winter
td: flower early - flower months in advance (T3)

pairs.q: If ash trees come into leaf before oak trees, the weather in [ANSWER] (summer) will probably be wet
pairs.p: A cold spring will see the opposite. As the British know only too well, a colder summer is likely to be a rain-soaked one
td: come into leaf before - leafing before (T2)
probably be wet - likely to be rain-soaked (T3)

pairs.q: The research was carried out using a particular species of [ANSWER] (mustard plant)
pairs.p: The work was done in a model system, using a mustard plant called Arabidopsis
td: research was carried out - work was done (T2)
particular species - a mustard plant called Arabidopsis (T4)

pairs.q: The counter-excavation method completely replaced the qanat method in the 6th century BCE
pairs.p: a second method of tunnel construction appeared called the counter-excavation method... used to cut through high mountains when the qanat method was not a practical alternative
td: completely replaced - appeared as a second method when qanat was not practical (T6)

pairs.q: Information about a problem that occurred during the construction of the Saldae aqueduct system was found in an ancient book
pairs.p: An inscription written on the side of a 428-meter tunnel, built by the Romans as part of the Saldae aqueduct system
td: found in an ancient book - inscription written on the side of the tunnel (T3)

pairs.q: The mistake made by the builders of the Saldae aqueduct system was that the two parts of the tunnel failed to meet
pairs.p: describes how the two teams of builders missed each other in the mountain and how the later construction of a lateral link between both corridors corrected the initial error
td: two parts of the tunnel failed to meet - two teams missed each other (T3)

pairs.q: What type of mineral were the Dolaucothi mines in Wales built to extract?
pairs.p: Traces of such tunnels used to mine gold can still be found at the Dolaucothi mines in Wales
td: built to extract - used to mine (T2)

pairs.q: In addition to the patron, whose name might be carved onto a tunnel?
pairs.p: Most tunnels had inscriptions showing the names of patrons who ordered construction and sometimes the name of the architect

pairs.q: What part of Seleuceia Pieria was the Çevlik tunnel built to protect?
pairs.p: the 1.4-kilometer Cevlik tunnel in Turkey, built to divert the floodwater threatening the harbor of the ancient city of Seleuceia Pieria
td: built to protect - built to divert floodwater threatening (T3)

pairs.q: The writer's main point in the first paragraph is that our use of technology is having a hidden effect on us
pairs.p: an invisible, game-changing transformation links everyone in this picture: the neuronal circuit that underlies the brain's ability to read is subtly, rapidly changing

pairs.q: Turkle's main point: we should pay attention to what might be lost when innovation occurs
pairs.p: we do not err as a society when we innovate but when we ignore what we disrupt or diminish while innovating
td: pay attention to what might be lost - ignore what we disrupt or diminish (T6)

pairs.q: Some brain circuits adjust to whatever is required of them
pairs.p: it will adapt to that environment's requirements
td: adjust to whatever is required - adapt to requirements (T2)

pairs.q: The attitude of college students has influenced what they select to read
pairs.p: many college students actively avoid the classic literature of the 19th and 20th centuries in favour of something simpler
td: influenced what they select to read - avoid classic literature in favour of something simpler (T3)

pairs.q: Studies on digital screen use show some [ANSWER] (worrying) trends
pairs.p: digital screen use may be causing a variety of troubling downstream effects on reading comprehension

pairs.q: Mangen's team used a question & answer technique to find out how [ANSWER] (thorough) each group's understanding of the plot was
pairs.p: Mangen's group asked subjects questions about a short story... students who read on print were superior in their comprehension
td: how thorough the understanding was - how comprehension compared (T3)

pairs.q: Those who read screens found the order of information [ANSWER] (hard) to recall
pairs.p: Results indicated that students who read on print were superior in their comprehension to screen-reading peers, particularly in their ability to sequence detail and reconstruct the plot in chronological order
td: hard to recall - screen readers were inferior, print readers superior in sequencing (T6)
order of information - sequence detail, chronological order (T3)

pairs.q: Studies show that students are tending to read [ANSWER] (isolated) words and phrases in a text to save time
pairs.p: the 'new norm' in reading is skimming, involving word-spotting and browsing through the text. Many readers now use a pattern when reading in which they sample the first line and then word-spot through the rest
td: read isolated words and phrases - word-spotting and browsing (T3)

pairs.q: This approach gives the reader a superficial understanding of the [ANSWER] (emotional) content of material, leaving no time for thought
pairs.p: we don't have time to grasp complexity, to understand another's feelings, to perceive beauty, and to create thoughts of the reader's own
td: superficial understanding - don't have time to grasp (T3)

pairs.q: The medium we use to read can affect our choice of reading content
pairs.p: how we all have begun to read on various mediums and how that changes not only what we read, but also the purposes for which we read
td: choice of reading content - what we read (T2)

pairs.q: Some age groups are more likely to lose their complex reading skills than others
pairs.p: The subtle atrophy of critical analysis and empathy affects us all equally
td: some age groups more likely - affects us all equally (T6)
complex reading skills - critical analysis and empathy (T3)

pairs.q: We still have opportunities to rectify the problems that technology is presenting
pairs.p: We possess both the science and the technology to identify and redress the changes in how we read before they become entrenched
td: opportunities to rectify - possess the means to redress (T2)

pairs.q: Heading: The superiority of AI projections over those made by humans (Section A)
pairs.p: Many decisions in our lives require a good forecast, and AI is almost always better at forecasting than we are

pairs.q: Heading: Widespread distrust of an AI innovation (Section B — Watson for Oncology)
pairs.p: this has caused even more suspicion and disbelief, leading many doctors to ignore the seemingly outlandish AI recommendations

pairs.q: Heading: Reasons why we have more faith in human judgement than in AI (Section C)
pairs.p: Trust in other people is often based on our understanding of how others think and having experience of their reliability
td: faith in human judgement - trust in other people (T2)
reasons why - based on understanding and experience (T3)

pairs.q: Heading: An increasing divergence of attitudes towards AI (Section D)
pairs.p: Optimists became more extreme in their enthusiasm for AI and sceptics became even more guarded
td: increasing divergence of attitudes - optimists more extreme, sceptics even more guarded (T3)

pairs.q: Heading: Encouraging openness about how AI functions (Section E)
pairs.p: Another solution may be to reveal more about the algorithms which AI uses and the purposes they serve
td: how AI functions - algorithms AI uses (T3)

pairs.q: Heading: The advantages of involving users in AI processes (Section F)
pairs.p: allowing people some control over AI decision-making could also improve trust and enable AI to learn from human experience
td: advantages - improve trust, enable AI to learn (T2)

pairs.q: Section A is highlighting the existence of a problem
pairs.p: Recent cases show that people don't like relying on AI and prefer to trust human experts, even if these experts are wrong
td: highlighting the existence of a problem - people don't like relying on AI (T3)

pairs.q: AI's complexity makes people feel that they are at a disadvantage
pairs.p: AI's decision-making process is usually too difficult for most people to comprehend. And interacting with something we don't understand can cause anxiety and give us a sense that we're losing control
td: complexity makes people feel at a disadvantage - too difficult to comprehend, causes anxiety, losing control (T3)

pairs.q: The media leads the public to be mistrustful of AI
pairs.p: Embarrassing AI failures receive a disproportionate amount of media attention, emphasising the message that we cannot rely on technology
td: the media leads - media attention emphasising (T2)

pairs.q: Subjective depictions of AI in sci-fi films make people change their opinions about automation
pairs.p: regardless of whether the film they watched depicted AI in a positive or negative light, simply watching a cinematic vision of our technological future polarised the participants' attitudes
td: make people change - regardless of depiction, it polarised rather than changed direction (T6)

pairs.q: Rejection of the possibilities of AI may have a negative effect on many people's lives
pairs.p: refusing to accept the advantages offered by AI could place a large group of people at a serious disadvantage
td: rejection of possibilities - refusing to accept advantages (T2)

pairs.q: Familiarity with AI has very little impact on people's attitudes to the technology
pairs.p: Simply having previous experience with AI can significantly improve people's opinions about the technology

pairs.q: AI applications which users are able to modify are more likely to gain consumer approval
pairs.p: when people were allowed the freedom to slightly modify an algorithm, they felt more satisfied with its decisions, more likely to believe it was superior and more likely to use it in the future
td: gain consumer approval - felt more satisfied, more likely to use (T3)

ng.q: Playing with dolls was found to benefit girls' writing more than boys' writing
ng.trap: Passage mentions 'dolls' and 'writing' but never compares girls vs boys. Students see 'dolls' and assume 'girls' from cultural stereotype.
ng.trap_passage: Children wrote longer and better-structured stories when they first played with dolls representing characters in the story.

ng.q: Staff should be allowed to choose when they take breaks during the working day
ng.trap: Passage says 'allowing adequate breaks' but says nothing about staff CHOOSING WHEN to take them. The question adds information not in the passage.
ng.trap_passage: Actions that can be adopted include allowing adequate breaks during the working day, staff functions that involve families.

ng.q: Henderson and Notman were surprised by the results of their 1865 experiment
ng.trap: Passage mentions the 1865 experiment but says nothing about whether they were surprised by the results. No emotional reaction is described.
ng.trap_passage: The two men made a photographic excursion to Niagara Falls in 1860 and they cooperated on experiments with magnesium flares in 1865.

ng.q: The studio that Henderson opened in 1866 was close to his home
ng.trap: Passage says he opened a studio in 1866 but gives zero information about where it was located relative to his home.
ng.trap_passage: In 1866, he gave up his business to open a photographic studio, advertising himself as a portrait and landscape photographer.

ng.q: Slow language development in children is likely to prove disappointing to their parents
ng.trap: Passage discusses parents' theories about language development timing, but never mentions whether parents feel disappointed. No emotional reaction is stated.
ng.trap_passage: Parents' implicit theories of their children's language development will determine at what ages they will be willing to make corrections in their children's speech.

ng.q: Play helps children to develop their artistic talents
ng.trap: Passage lists cognitive, social, emotional, physical development through play but never specifically mentions artistic talents. Students may confuse 'creative' with 'artistic'.
ng.trap_passage: Through play, children learn to regulate their behavior, lay foundations for science and mathematics, figure out social relationships, build creative problem-solving skills.

ng.q: The recent studies of bees used different methods of measuring age-related decline
ng.trap: Passage says results were 'mixed' but never explains whether different methods were used. Students assume 'mixed results' means 'different methods'.
ng.trap_passage: Scientists have looked at some similar aspects in bees, but the results of recent bee studies were mixed — some showed age-related declines and others didn't.

ng.q: Improvements in the quality of TV wildlife documentaries have resulted in increased numbers of zoo visitors
ng.trap: Passage mentions TV documentaries as alternative to zoos, but never says documentaries affected zoo visitor numbers. Students connect two separate ideas.
ng.trap_passage: Television documentaries are becoming ever more detailed and impressive, and many natural history specimens are on display in museums.

ng.q: Studying animals in zoos is less stressful for the animals than studying them in the wild
ng.trap: Passage says 'less risk' for research but 'risk' refers to research risk/variables, not stress to the animals themselves. Students misread 'risk' as 'stress'.
ng.trap_passage: Being able to undertake research on animals in zoos where there is less risk and fewer variables means real changes can be effected.

ng.q: The creatures most in danger from ocean trash are certain seabirds
ng.trap: Passage uses seabirds as ONE EXAMPLE but never claims they are the MOST endangered. Students read an example as a superlative.
ng.trap_passage: A study could show that certain seabirds eat plastic bags, and go on to warn that whole bird populations are at risk of dying out.

ng.q: Some mussels choose to eat plastic in preference to their natural diet
ng.trap: Passage says mussels ingested plastic, but never says they CHOSE to eat it or PREFERRED it over food. Students confuse 'ingested' with 'chose to eat'.
ng.trap_passage: The plastic moved from the mussels' stomachs to their bloodstreams, and stayed there for weeks — but didn't seem to stress out the shellfish.

ng.q: Beacons and flashing lights are still used by the ATC today
ng.trap: Passage describes beacons and lights as historical tools from the 1920s but never says whether they are still used today.
ng.trap_passage: As early as the 1920s, the earliest air traffic controllers manually guided aircraft using lights and flags, while beacons and flashing lights were placed along routes.

ng.q: Pilkington invested some of his own money in his float plant
ng.trap: Passage says Pilkington convinced his COMPANY to build the plant, but never mentions whether he invested his OWN PERSONAL money.
ng.trap_passage: Pilkington built a pilot plant in 1953 and by 1955 he had convinced his company to build a full-scale plant.

ng.q: Weather forecasters are intensely interested in Diels's system
ng.trap: Passage mentions 'interactive meteorology' as Diels's hope, but never says weather FORECASTERS are interested. Students confuse 'meteorology' with 'meteorologists'.
ng.trap_passage: Diels hopes to see the birth of 'interactive meteorology' — not just forecasting the weather but controlling it.

ng.q: To be a genius is worth the high personal cost
ng.trap: Passage describes the cost/price of genius but never gives an opinion on whether the cost is WORTH IT. That's a value judgment the passage doesn't make.
ng.trap_passage: The price of sudden climate change, in famine, disease and suffering, was often high.

ng.q: Within seven years, about 90% of a human body is replaced as new
ng.trap: Passage says bodies continuously renew material but never gives a specific timeframe (7 years) or percentage (90%). Students may have heard these numbers elsewhere and assume the passage confirms them.
ng.trap_passage: Our bodies continuously exchange old substance for new, just like a spring which maintains its form but in which the water molecules are always different.

ng.q: Michael Faraday suggested Perkin should enroll in the Royal College of Chemistry
ng.trap_passage: His talent and devotion to the subject were perceived by his teacher, Thomas Hall, who encouraged him to attend a series of lectures given by the eminent scientist Michael Faraday at the Royal Institution.
ng.trap: Passage says Hall encouraged attending Faraday's lectures, not that Faraday suggested enrollment at the Royal College. Students confuse 'attend lectures' with 'enroll in college' and 'Hall encouraged' with 'Faraday suggested'.

ng.q: The trees from which quinine is derived grow only in South America
ng.trap_passage: The drug is derived from the bark of the cinchona tree, native to South America
ng.trap: Passage says 'native to South America' but never says 'only'. Students equate 'native to' with 'found exclusively in'. The passage does not confirm or deny whether the tree grows elsewhere.

ng.q: Perkin was inspired by the discoveries of the famous scientist Louis Pasteur
ng.trap_passage: proving the truth of the famous scientist Louis Pasteur's words 'chance favors only the prepared mind'
ng.trap: Passage mentions Pasteur's words (a quote), not his discoveries. Students see 'Louis Pasteur' and 'famous scientist' and assume the statement is confirmed. The passage never says Pasteur's discoveries inspired Perkin.

ng.q: The Americans and Australians have co-operated on joint research projects
ng.trap_passage: So far there have been a number of searches by various groups around the world, including Australian searches using the radio telescope at Parkes, New South Wales.
ng.trap: Passage mentions both American (NASA, Arecibo) and Australian searches separately, but never says they co-operated on joint projects. Students see both countries mentioned and assume collaboration.

ng.q: The NASA project attracted criticism from some members of Congress
ng.trap_passage: the US Congress voted NASA $10 million per year for ten years to conduct a thorough search for extra-terrestrial life
ng.trap: Passage mentions Congress voting to fund the project but says nothing about criticism. Students might assume that a vote implies debate/criticism, but the passage only states the funding decision.

ng.q: Turtles were among the first group of animals to migrate back to the sea
ng.trap_passage: Turtles went back to the sea a very long time ago and, like all vertebrate returnees to the water, they breathe air.
ng.trap: Passage says turtles went back 'a very long time ago' but never compares their timing to other animals or says they were 'among the first'. Students equate 'a very long time ago' with 'first'.

ng.q: Early astronomers suspected that the atmosphere on Venus was toxic
ng.trap_passage: Venus exhibits a halo of light when it is seen just outside the Sun's disc. While this showed astronomers that Venus was surrounded by a thick layer of gases refracting sunlight around it
ng.trap: Passage mentions Venus has a thick layer of gases but never mentions toxicity. Students see 'thick layer of gases' and assume toxic atmosphere is implied.

ng.q: Most people are too shy to try different things
ng.trap_passage: Observation of iconoclasts shows that they embrace novelty while most people avoid things that are different.
ng.trap: Passage says most people 'avoid things that are different' but never says this is because of shyness. Students confuse avoidance with shyness. The passage attributes avoidance to brain shortcuts and fear, not shyness.

ng.q: When concern about embarrassment matters less, other fears become irrelevant
ng.trap_passage: the two that inhibit iconoclastic thinking and people generally find difficult to deal with are fear of uncertainty and fear of public ridicule
ng.trap: Passage lists fear of uncertainty and fear of public ridicule as two separate inhibitors but never says removing one makes the other irrelevant. Students assume if one fear decreases, others follow.

ng.q: Prescriptive grammar books cost a lot of money to buy in the 18th century
ng.trap_passage: All the main languages have been studied prescriptively, especially in the 18th-century approach to the writing of grammars and dictionaries.
ng.trap: Passage mentions 18th-century grammar books existed but says nothing about their cost. Students see '18th century' and 'grammars' and assume cost information is present.

ng.q: Marie became interested in science when she was a child
ng.trap_passage: From childhood, Marie was remarkable for her prodigious memory, and at the age of 16 won a gold medal on completion of her secondary education.
ng.trap: Passage mentions 'from childhood' about her memory, not about interest in science specifically. Students see 'from childhood' and assume it refers to scientific interest.

ng.q: Marie's sister Bronia studied the medical uses of radioactivity
ng.trap_passage: she was able to finance her sister Bronia's medical studies in Paris
ng.trap: Passage says Bronia did 'medical studies' but never specifies radioactivity. Students confuse Bronia's medical studies with Marie's radioactivity work. Later, Bronia became director of the Radium Institute in Warsaw, but the passage doesn't say she studied radioactivity's medical uses.

ng.q: More people visit museums than theme parks
ng.trap_passage: they are also operating in a very competitive environment, where visitors make choices on how and where to spend their free time
ng.trap: Passage mentions competition between museums and theme parks for visitors but never compares actual visitor numbers. Students assume competitive environment implies a ranking.

ng.q: The few existing stepwells in Delhi are more attractive than those found elsewhere
ng.trap_passage: while a few also survive in Delhi
ng.trap: Passage mentions stepwells exist in Delhi but makes no comparison of attractiveness. Students see 'a few' and 'Delhi' and assume the passage comments on their quality.

ng.q: It took workers many years to build the stone steps characteristic of stepwells
ng.trap_passage: stepwells comprise a series of stone steps descending from ground level to the water source
ng.trap: Passage describes the structure of stepwells but never mentions how long they took to build. Students see 'stone steps' and assume construction time is discussed.

ng.q: Cars are prohibitively expensive in some EU candidate countries
ng.trap_passage: many candidate countries inherited a transport system which encourages rail, the distribution between modes has tipped sharply in favour of road transport
ng.trap: Passage discusses EU candidate countries and transport but never mentions car prices. Students see candidate countries + road transport and assume cost is addressed.

ng.q: The Gothenburg European Council was set up 30 years ago
ng.trap_passage: This objective, agreed by the Gothenburg European Council, has to be achieved... in 30 years' time
ng.trap: Passage mentions '30 years' in connection to the transport goal timeline, not when the Council was established. Students confuse '30 years' time' (future target) with age of the Council.

ng.q: The physical surroundings in which a person works play a key role in determining their creativity
ng.trap_passage: there are people working in luxurious, state-of-the-art centres designed to stimulate innovation who find that their environment doesn't make them feel at all creative
ng.trap: Passage explicitly CONTRADICTS this — people in great environments don't feel creative. This is NO/FALSE, not NG. Students might see 'environment' and 'creative' and mark TRUE.

ng.q: Teams work best when their members are of equally matched intelligence
ng.trap_passage: three people will be better than one at solving problems, even if that one person is the smartest person in the field
ng.trap: Passage discusses team size vs individual intelligence but never says members need equally matched intelligence. Students see 'intelligence' and 'team' and assume a match is confirmed.

ng.q: It is easier for smaller companies to be innovative
ng.trap_passage: there are those who don't have a budget, or much space, but who innovate successfully
ng.trap: Passage mentions people with limited resources innovating, but never compares small vs large companies. Students equate 'don't have a budget' with 'smaller companies'.

ng.q: Tourism contributes over six per cent of the Australian gross national product
ng.trap_passage: This industry is the world's leading industrial contributor, producing over 6 per cent of the world's national product
ng.trap: Passage says 6% of the WORLD'S national product, not Australia's specifically. Students see '6 per cent' and 'national product' and assume it matches.

ng.q: Tourism has a social impact because it promotes recreation
ng.trap_passage: tourism has a profound impact both on the world economy and, because of the educative effect of travel and the effects on employment, on society itself
ng.trap: Passage mentions social impact but attributes it to 'educative effect' and 'employment', not recreation. Students see 'social impact' and assume recreation is mentioned.

ng.q: Visitor spending is always greater than the spending of residents in tourist areas
ng.trap_passage: Since many of these businesses also serve local residents, the impact of spending by visitors can easily be overlooked or underestimated
ng.trap: Passage mentions both visitor and resident spending but never compares their amounts. Students see both mentioned and assume a comparison is made.

ng.q: Art history should focus on discovering the meaning of art using a range of media
ng.trap_passage: the dominant critical approach becomes that of the art historian, a specialised academic approach devoted to 'discovering the meaning' of art within the cultural context of its time
ng.trap: Passage mentions art historians 'discovering meaning' but says nothing about 'a range of media'. Students see 'discovering the meaning' and assume the full statement is supported.

ng.q: Reproductions of fine art should only be sold to the public if they are of high quality
ng.trap_passage: If appropriate works of fine art could be rendered permanently accessible to the public by means of high-fidelity reproductions
ng.trap: Passage mentions 'high-fidelity reproductions' but doesn't say reproductions should ONLY be sold if high quality. Students see 'high-fidelity' and equate it with the condition in the question.

ng.q: Leaves which turn colours other than red are more likely to be damaged by sunlight
ng.trap_passage: why some trees resort to producing red pigments while others don't bother, and simply reveal their orange or yellow hues. Do these trees have other means at their disposal to prevent overexposure?
ng.trap: Passage asks whether non-red trees have other protection but never confirms they are MORE likely to be damaged. Students see the question posed in the passage and assume it's answered.

ng.q: The Lapita learnt to predict the duration of El Ninos
ng.trap_passage: El Nino... may have helped scatter the Lapita... a series of unusually frequent El Ninos around the time of the Lapita expansion
ng.trap: Passage mentions El Ninos affecting Lapita but never says they learned to predict them. Students see El Ninos + Lapita and assume prediction is discussed.

ng.q: It is likely that the majority of Lapita settled on Fiji
ng.trap_passage: they encountered hundreds of islands – more than 300 in Fiji alone
ng.trap: Passage mentions 300+ islands in Fiji but never says majority of Lapita settled there. Students see 'more than 300 in Fiji' and assume most people settled there.

ng.q: More firefighters have been hired to improve fire-fighting capacity
ng.trap_passage: funding for firefighting has increased, despite huge cuts in many other programs
ng.trap: Passage mentions increased funding but never specifically says more firefighters were hired. Students equate 'increased funding' with 'hiring more firefighters'.

ng.q: Wagner was the first person to do research on South American lizards
ng.trap_passage: biologist Gunter Wagner of Yale University reported some work on the evolutionary history of a group of South American lizards called Bachia
ng.trap: Passage says Wagner researched Bachia lizards but never says he was the first. Students see his research described and assume he was first.

ng.q: China's transport system was not suitable for industry in the 18th century
ng.trap_passage: China also had many of these factors
ng.trap: Passage mentions China had many factors for industrialisation but never discusses China's transport system specifically. Students see China mentioned and assume transport is addressed.

ng.q: People in Britain used to make beer at home
ng.trap_passage: We drank beer. For a long time, the English were protected by the strong antibacterial agent in hops
ng.trap: Passage discusses beer consumption but never mentions home brewing. Students see 'we drank beer' and assume home production is discussed.

ng.q: Methods for predicting the Earth's population have recently changed
ng.trap_passage: Applying the most conservative estimates to current demographic trends, the human population will increase by about three billion people by then.
ng.trap: The passage mentions 'current demographic trends' and 'conservative estimates', but never says these methods have recently changed. Students confuse 'current' with 'recently changed'.

ng.q: There was some opposition to the design of the Falkirk Wheel at first
ng.trap_passage: Numerous ideas were submitted for the project, including concepts ranging from rolling eggs to tilting tanks, from giant seesaws to overhead monorails.
ng.trap: The passage mentions many competing ideas but never says there was opposition to the winning design. Students confuse 'many alternatives' with 'opposition'.

ng.q: The Falkirk Wheel is the only boat lift in the world which has steel sections bolted together by hand
ng.trap_passage: Over 45,000 bolt holes were matched with their bolts, and each bolt was hand-tightened.
ng.trap: The passage confirms hand-tightening but never claims this is the ONLY boat lift with this feature. Students see 'hand-tightened' and assume uniqueness, but the passage only says the Wheel is the only ROTATING boat lift.

ng.q: The Mary Rose was the only ship to sink in the battle of 19 July 1545
ng.trap_passage: On 19 July 1545, English and French fleets were engaged in a sea battle off the coast of southern England in the area of water called the Solent.
ng.trap: The passage describes the battle and the Mary Rose sinking, but never says it was the ONLY ship to sink. Students assume because only this ship is named, no others sank.

ng.q: Forsythe's findings contradicted previous beliefs on the function of 'fractals' in art
ng.trap_passage: Alex Forsythe of the University of Liverpool analysed the visual intricacy of different pieces of art, and her results suggest that many artists use a key level of detail to please the brain.
ng.trap: The passage describes Forsythe's findings about fractals and art but never mentions whether these contradicted previous beliefs. Students assume new findings must contradict old ones.

ng.q: It is easier to find meaning in the field of science than in that of art
ng.trap_passage: In some ways, it's not so different to science, where we are constantly looking for systems and decoding meaning so that we can view and appreciate the world in a new way.
ng.trap: The passage compares art and science as similar processes of looking for meaning, but never says one is EASIER than the other. Students confuse 'similar process' with a comparison of difficulty.

ng.q: Silk yarn makes up the majority of silk currently exported from China
ng.trap_passage: China has gradually recaptured its position as the world's biggest producer and exporter of raw silk and silk yarn.
ng.trap: The passage mentions China exports 'raw silk and silk yarn' but never says which makes up the majority. Students see both terms mentioned together and assume yarn is the larger share.

ng.q: Very few experts agree that the movement of aphids can be considered migration
ng.trap_passage: daily vertical movements by zooplankton in the ocean...can also be considered migration. So can the movement of aphids
ng.trap: The passage says aphid movement CAN be considered migration but never says how many experts agree or disagree. Students confuse 'can be considered' with widespread agreement or disagreement.

ng.q: Twins are at greater risk of developing certain illnesses than non-twins
ng.trap_passage: researchers can quantify the extent to which our genes affect our lives. If identical twins are more similar to each other with respect to an ailment than fraternal twins are, then vulnerability to the disease must be rooted at least in part in heredity.
ng.trap: The passage discusses using twins to STUDY disease vulnerability but never says twins themselves are at greater risk. Students confuse 'studying disease through twins' with 'twins having more disease'.

ng.q: Bouchard advertised in newspapers for twins who had been separated at birth
ng.trap_passage: Over two decades 137 sets of twins eventually visited Thomas Bouchard's lab in what became known as the Minnesota Study of Twins Reared Apart.
ng.trap: The passage says twins 'visited Bouchard's lab' but never specifies HOW they were recruited. Students assume advertising in newspapers, but the passage gives no recruitment method.

ng.q: Background music has more effect on some people than on others
ng.trap_passage: Usually not meant to be noticeable, it often provides a tone or an emotional attitude toward the story and/or the characters depicted.
ng.trap: The passage describes what background music DOES but never compares its effect on different people. Students assume individual variation must be mentioned.

ng.q: A complex idea can be explained more clearly in a sentence than in a single word
ng.trap_passage: One of the showiest stunts that some languages can pull off is an ability to build up words of breath-breaking length, and thus express in one word what English takes a whole sentence to say.
ng.trap: The passage compares a single word vs a sentence for expressing ideas, but never says which is CLEARER. Students assume length equals clarity.

ng.q: The cork oak has the thickest bark of any living tree
ng.trap_passage: Its bark grows up to 20 cm in thickness, insulating the tree like a coat wrapped around the trunk and branches.
ng.trap: The passage gives the bark thickness (20 cm) but never compares it to other trees. Students see 'up to 20 cm' and assume it must be the thickest.

ng.q: The number of people buying dolls has grown over the centuries
ng.trap_passage: people who collect dolls may go beyond simply enlarging their collection, and develop an interest in the way that dolls are made, or the materials that are used.
ng.trap: The passage discusses doll collecting and doll-making materials over centuries, but never says the NUMBER of collectors has grown. Students confuse 'dolls changed over centuries' with 'more collectors over centuries'.

ng.q: Arranging a stamp collection by the size of the stamps is less common than other methods
ng.trap_passage: Stamps collectors arrange their stamps in albums, usually very neatly, organising their collection according to certain commonplace principles – perhaps by country in alphabetical order, or grouping stamps by what they depict.
ng.trap: The passage mentions organising by country or by what they depict, but never mentions organising by SIZE or compares methods by popularity. Students notice size is absent from the list and assume it must be less common.

ng.q: It is difficult to attract students onto courses that do not focus on a career
ng.trap_passage: A student can prepare for a career in resort management, engineering, interior design, accounting, music, law enforcement, you name it.
ng.trap: The passage lists many career-focused courses but never says non-career courses have difficulty attracting students. Students assume that because career courses are listed, non-career courses must struggle.

ng.q: Bingham returned to Machu Picchu in order to find evidence to support his theory
ng.trap_passage: Bingham made desperate attempts to prove this belief for nearly 40 years.
ng.trap: The passage says Bingham tried to prove his belief for 40 years but never says he RETURNED to Machu Picchu for this purpose. Students confuse 'desperate attempts to prove' with 'returned to the site'.

ng.q: Bilingual people are better than monolingual people at guessing correctly what words are before they are finished
ng.trap_passage: Long before the word is finished, the brain's language system begins to guess what that word might be.
ng.trap: The passage describes how the brain guesses words before they finish, but never says bilinguals guess MORE CORRECTLY. Students confuse 'both languages activate' with 'better guessing'.

ng.q: Fewer bilingual people than monolingual people suffer from brain disease in old age
ng.trap_passage: bilingual patients reported showing initial symptoms of the disease an average of five years later than monolingual patients.
ng.trap: The passage says symptoms appear LATER in bilinguals, not that FEWER bilinguals get the disease. Students confuse 'delayed onset' with 'lower incidence'.

ng.q: Nowadays, most glass is produced by large international manufacturers
ng.trap_passage: It has become a modern, hi-tech industry operating in a fiercely competitive global market where quality, design and service levels are critical.
ng.trap: The passage says glass-making is a hi-tech global industry, but never says MOST glass is produced by large international manufacturers. Students confuse 'global market' with 'large international manufacturers'.

ng.q: The large growth in the European lynx population since 1970 has exceeded conservationists' expectations
ng.trap_passage: The European population has tripled since 1970 to roughly 10,000.
ng.trap: The passage gives the population growth data but never mentions conservationists' expectations or whether the growth exceeded them. Students see 'tripled' and assume it must have been surprising.

ng.q: Banks have been mismanaged to a greater extent than other businesses
ng.trap_passage: The role of bank directors and management and their widely publicised failures have been extensively picked over and examined in reports.
ng.trap: The passage discusses bank failures but never compares the EXTENT of mismanagement between banks and other businesses. Students see 'widely publicised failures' and assume banks were worse.

ng.q: It was found that most visitors started searching on the website by geographical location
ng.trap_passage: On the website, visitors can search for activities not solely by geographical location, but also by the particular nature of the activity
ng.trap: The passage mentions 'geographical location' as one search option, which tricks students into thinking it confirms the statement. But the passage only describes what search options EXIST — it never says most visitors STARTED with geographical location or preferred it.

ng.q: Visitors like staying in small hotels in New Zealand rather than in larger ones
ng.trap_passage: visitors to New Zealand don't want to be 'one of the crowd' and find activities that involve only a few people more special and meaningful
ng.trap: Students see 'small' vs 'large' theme and 'few people' and assume this confirms a preference for small hotels. But the passage is talking about ACTIVITIES (preferring activities with few people), not HOTELS. The passage never mentions hotel size at all.

ng.q: The non-experts in Moffat's study all responded in a predictable way
ng.trap_passage: He asked both expert musicians and non-experts to assess six compositions... People who thought the composer was a computer tended to dislike the piece more than those who believed it was human. This was true even among the experts
ng.trap: Students see 'non-experts' mentioned in the passage and read about the general pattern of responses. They assume 'predictable' matches the described pattern. But the passage never says ALL non-experts responded the same way — 'tended to' describes a general trend, not a universal response. The word 'all' in the question makes this NOT GIVEN.

ng.q: The trees planted by the Dutch produced larger quantities of cinnamon than the wild trees
ng.trap_passage: the Dutch began cultivating their own cinnamon trees to supplement the diminishing number of wild trees available for use
ng.trap: Students see 'cultivating their own trees' and 'wild trees' mentioned together and assume the passage compares their output. But the passage only says the Dutch grew trees to SUPPLEMENT (add to) the shrinking wild supply — it never says which type produced MORE cinnamon.

ng.q: Coconuts found on the west coast of America were a different type from those found on the east coast
ng.trap_passage: coconuts discovered along the west coast of America by 16th century sailors has been the subject of centuries of discussion. Two diametrically opposed origins have been proposed
ng.trap: Students see 'west coast' and 'east coast' both mentioned and assume the passage compares the coconut types. But the passage only discusses the ORIGIN (where they came from) of west coast coconuts, not whether they were a different TYPE from east coast ones. The distinction between origin and type is the trap.

ng.q: Coconuts are cultivated in different ways in America and the Pacific
ng.trap_passage: coconuts originated on coral islands in the Pacific and were dispersed from there
ng.trap: Students see 'America', 'Pacific', and 'cultivated' all appear in the passage and assume the passage compares cultivation methods. But the passage only discusses where coconuts ORIGINATED and were DISPERSED TO — it never mentions or compares HOW they were cultivated in different regions.

ng.q: The VOC was the world's first major trading company
ng.trap_passage: In 1602, Dutch merchants founded the VOC, a trading corporation better known as the Dutch East India Company. By 1617, the VOC was the richest commercial operation in the world.
ng.trap: Passage says VOC was the richest commercial operation but never says it was the first major trading company. Students confuse 'richest' with 'first'.

ng.q: Participants in the Santa Cruz study were more accurate at identifying the laughs of friends than those of strangers
ng.trap_passage: Participants were asked whether they thought the people laughing were friends or strangers. On average, the results were remarkably consistent: worldwide, people's guesses were correct approximately 60% of the time.
ng.trap: Passage gives overall accuracy (60%) but never compares accuracy for friend-laughs versus stranger-laughs. Students see the 60% and assume it answers the question.

ng.q: When Moore started at the Royal College of Art, its reputation for teaching sculpture was excellent
ng.trap_passage: At the end of that year, he passed the sculpture examination and was awarded a scholarship to the Royal College of Art in London.
ng.trap: Passage mentions Moore going to the Royal College but says nothing about its reputation for teaching. Students assume a scholarship implies excellence.

ng.q: The Trocadero Museum's Mayan sculpture attracted a lot of public interest
ng.trap_passage: When he visited the Trocadero Museum in Paris, he was impressed by a cast of a Mayan sculpture of the rain spirit.
ng.trap: Passage describes Moore being impressed by the sculpture, but says nothing about public interest. Students confuse Moore's personal reaction with general public attention.

ng.q: Local families have told Whaley about some traditional uses of huarango products
ng.trap_passage: he has been working with local families to attempt to create a sustainable income from the huarangos by turning their products into foodstuffs
ng.trap: Passage says Whaley works with local families to create income from huarango products, but never says families told him about traditional uses. Students confuse 'working with families' with 'learning from families'.

ng.q: Whaley has plans to go to Africa to set up a similar project
ng.trap_passage: He sees his project as a model that has the potential to be rolled out across other arid areas around the world. 'If we can do it here...that's a real message of hope for lots of places, including Africa'
ng.trap: Passage mentions Africa as a place that could benefit but never says Whaley has plans to go there. Students confuse 'message of hope for Africa' with 'plans to go to Africa'.

ng.q: La Gomera is the most mountainous of all the Canary Islands
ng.trap_passage: This small volcanic island is mountainous, with steep rocky slopes and deep, wooded ravines, rising to 1,487 metres at its highest peak.
ng.trap: Passage describes La Gomera as mountainous but never compares it to other Canary Islands. Students see 'mountainous' and assume it must be the most mountainous.

ng.q: There is a contrast between the moral principles of different businesses
ng.trap_passage: My views may seem to ignore the belief that businesses should act in accordance with moral principles even if this leads to a reduction in their profits.
ng.trap: Passage discusses moral principles in general terms but never compares the moral principles of different businesses. Students see 'moral principles' and assume a comparison is being made.

ng.q: In the future, businesses will show more concern for the environment
ng.trap_passage: I predict that in the future, just as in the past, changes in public attitudes will be essential for changes in businesses' environmental practices.
ng.trap: Passage says public attitudes will need to change for businesses to change, but never predicts that businesses will show more concern. Students confuse 'public attitudes are essential' with 'businesses will change'.

ng.q: Liu and colleagues were the first researchers to compare polar bears and brown bears genetically
ng.trap_passage: A 2014 study by Shi Ping Liu and colleagues sheds light on this mystery. They compared the genetic structure of polar bears with that of their closest relatives from a warmer climate, the brown bears.
ng.trap: Passage says Liu compared polar bears and brown bears genetically, but never says they were the FIRST to do so. Students see the comparison mentioned and assume it was pioneering.

ng.q: There are more geoglyphs in the shape of a horse than any other creature
ng.trap_passage: The figures include giants, horses, crosses and regimental badges.
ng.trap: Passage lists different types of figures including horses, but never says which shape is most common. Students see 'horses' listed and the passage is about a horse, so they assume horses are the most common.

ng.q: The fame of the Uffington White Horse is due to its size
ng.trap_passage: The Uffington White Horse is a unique, stylised representation of a horse consisting of a long, sleek back, thin disjointed legs, a streaming tail, and a bird-like beaked head.
ng.trap: Passage says the horse is 'the most famous' and describes its unique style, but never says its fame is due to its size. Students assume fame = large size because it is a huge geoglyph.

ng.q: Skilled craftsmen were needed for the mortise and tenon method of fixing planks
ng.trap_passage: Starting from the 6th century BCE, they were fixed using a method called mortise and tenon, whereby one plank locked into another without the need for stitching.
ng.trap: Passage describes the mortise and tenon method but never mentions whether skilled craftsmen were specifically needed for it. Students assume a technical method requires skilled craftsmen.

ng.q: Only experienced builders were employed to construct a tunnel using the counter-excavation method
ng.trap_passage: This method required greater planning and advanced knowledge of surveying, mathematics and geometry as both ends of a tunnel had to meet correctly at the center of the mountain.
ng.trap: Passage says the method required advanced knowledge but never says 'only experienced builders' were employed. Students see 'advanced knowledge' and assume only experienced workers were used.

ng.q: Yong's book contains more case studies than are necessary
ng.trap_passage: Among the less appealing case studies is one about a fungus that is wiping out entire populations of frogs... Another is about squid that carry luminescent bacteria
ng.trap: Passage describes several case studies but never makes a judgement about whether there are too many. Students may infer 'less appealing' means unnecessary, but it's about content not quantity.

ng.q: Efforts to control dengue fever have been surprisingly successful
ng.trap_passage: in an attempt to stop mosquitoes spreading dengue fever — a disease that infects 400 million people a year — mosquitoes are being loaded with a bacterium to block the disease
ng.trap: Passage mentions efforts to stop dengue fever using bacteria, but never says whether these efforts have been successful. Students see the method described and assume success.

ng.q: Participants in the couples experiment were aware that they were taking part in a study about wise reasoning
ng.trap_passage: couples in long-term romantic relationships were instructed to visualize an unresolved relationship conflict either through the eyes of an outsider or from their own perspective
ng.trap: Passage says couples were instructed to visualize from different perspectives, but never says what they were told the study was about. Students assume participants knew the research topic.

ng.q: In the couples experiment, the length of the couples' relationship had an impact on the results
ng.trap_passage: couples in long-term romantic relationships were instructed to visualize an unresolved relationship conflict
ng.trap: Passage mentions 'long-term relationships' as a selection criterion but never says relationship length affected the results. Students see 'long-term' mentioned and assume it was a variable.

ng.q: The Cambridge scientists' discovery of the 'thermometer molecule' caused surprise among other scientists
ng.trap_passage: An international team of scientists led by the University of Cambridge has discovered that the 'thermometer' molecule in plants enables them to develop according to seasonal temperature changes.
ng.trap: Passage describes the discovery but never mentions the reaction of other scientists. Students assume a major discovery would cause surprise.

ng.q: It may be possible to develop crops that require less water
ng.trap_passage: Discovering the molecules that allow plants to sense temperature has the potential to accelerate the breeding of crops resilient to thermal stress and climate change.
ng.trap: Passage mentions breeding resilient crops but specifically about thermal stress, never about water requirements. Students see 'resilient to climate change' and assume water is included.

ng.q: False information has become more widespread in today's digital era
ng.trap_passage: leaving us susceptible to false information and irrational ideas
ng.trap: Passage says we are susceptible to false information, but never explicitly says false information has become more widespread. Students see 'susceptible to false information' and assume it means it's more widespread.

ng.q: Portrayals of AI in media and entertainment are likely to become more positive
ng.trap_passage: As AI is represented more and more in media and entertainment, it could lead to a society split between those who benefit from AI and those who reject it.
ng.trap: Passage discusses increasing AI representation in media but never says portrayals will become more positive. Students see 'more and more' representation and assume it trends positive.

ng.q: Google was inspired to adopt flexibility by the success of General Electric
ng.trap_passage: Google and a number of other tech companies have embraced these kinds of flexible structures
ng.trap: This pair was incorrectly placed in pairs array with answer NOT GIVEN. The passage discusses flexibility but does not confirm Google was inspired by this specific success.
